title: Cherry clafoutis
subtitle: Baked French batter pudding with whole cherries and a light, custard-like crumb.
excerpt: Cherry clafoutis is a French baked pudding from the Limousin region: whole cherries baked inside a thin, eggy batter that sets firm on the outside and remains custardy in the centre. The batter is poured over the cherries raw; there is no pastry, no creaming, no folding. Thirty minutes in the oven and it is done.
sourceNotes: Clafoutis originates in the Limousin region of central France, where the local black cherries were traditionally baked whole, unpitted, in the batter. The pits are said to give the pudding a subtle almond flavour during baking, and leaving them in is still the traditional method. The name comes from the Occitan claufir, meaning to fill. The dish appears in French regional cookbooks from the nineteenth century and became more widely known in Britain through French cuisine's influence in the 1960s and 1970s. Variations using other stone fruit are technically clafoutis flaugnarde.

recipe.freezeNotes: Clafoutis does not freeze well; the batter becomes watery. Eat on the day or the following day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day, warm or at room temperature. The batter can be mixed up to two hours ahead and kept at room temperature.

recipeTools.notes: A 23 to 25 cm baking dish or gratin dish, about 4 cm deep, buttered.

content.text: Clafoutis is one of the simplest French desserts: cherries in a buttered dish, batter poured over, baked. The batter is very thin, halfway between a crepe batter and a custard. It puffs in the oven and settles as it cools. Serve warm with a dusting of icing sugar.

Traditionally the cherries are left whole with the stone in, which prevents the fruit from releasing too much juice and subtly flavours the batter. You can stone them if you prefer, but warn anyone eating that the pits are present if you go traditional.

items.prepNote: stalks removed; leave stones in for authenticity, or stone them

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional). Butter the baking dish generously. Scatter {{cherries}} in a single layer across the base.

Whisk {{eggs}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale. Add {{plain-flour}} and whisk until smooth. Gradually whisk in {{whole-milk}}, {{double-cream}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} until you have a thin, smooth batter.

Pour the batter gently over the cherries. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the clafoutis is puffed at the edges, set in the centre, and lightly golden on top. A skewer inserted in the middle should come out clean.
Leave to rest for 10 minutes before dusting with {{icing-sugar}} and serving. It will deflate as it cools; this is normal.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Clafoutis is very reliable. There are only a few things that can go wrong.

items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or dish too deep.
items.fix: Give it another 5 to 10 minutes and check again. The very centre should wobble slightly, like a set custard, not run. A shallower dish speeds the bake.

items.symptom: Clafoutis deflates dramatically and looks sunken.
items.fix: All clafoutis deflate once out of the oven. The puffed stage is the show; the settled stage is when you eat it. No action needed.
